nativeRange.habitat: Sluggish tributaries, backwaters, and large river systems with mud or sand substrate and abundant vegetation; also found in deep pools and hill streams of the Western Ghats

decorPreferences: large pieces of bogwood and driftwood for cover
smooth sand or fine-gravel substrate to protect barbels
rock caves and PVC pipe lengths as hiding refuges
tangles of beech branches or branchy wood
dim lighting or floating plants to reduce light intensity
robust or artificial plants (large specimens may uproot live plants)

diet.notes: Unspecialised predator feeding on fish, crustaceans, molluscs, aquatic insects, worms, and amphibians in the wild. In captivity accepts high-quality sinking carnivore pellets, frozen prawns, mussels, earthworms, and bloodworms. Live feeders are unnecessary. Feed at lights-out to suit the species’ nocturnal habits; remove uneaten food promptly given the large waste load.

goodWith: other large catfish of similar size
avoidWith: any fish small enough to fit in its mouth

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image; verify availability and license before use.

summary: The Sun Catfish is a large, brilliantly coloured nocturnal predator endemic to the Western Ghats of India. Its vivid orange-yellow body and bold black ‘bullseye’ shoulder spot make it one of the most striking large catfish in the hobby. It is listed as Endangered by the IUCN and requires a very large aquarium of at least 648 L.
careNotes: The Sun Catfish is an imposing species reaching up to 45 cm, with museum specimens recorded at that size and aquarium specimens commonly reaching 30 cm. A single specimen requires a minimum footprint of 180 cm × 60 cm (approximately 648 L / 170 US gal); a group of five or more demands considerably larger quarters. Efficient biological and mechanical filtration is essential to handle the heavy bioload produced at this size. Provide a soft sand substrate at least 5 cm deep to protect the four pairs of sensitive barbels during foraging; coarse gravel can abrade and infect barbel tissue. Dim lighting suits the species’ strictly nocturnal nature — use floating plants or caves to create dark refuges, and feed after lights-out to elicit natural feeding behaviour. Water should be maintained soft to moderately hard (gH 5–20) and slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.0–7.5), though the species is adaptable to harder and more alkaline conditions with acclimatisation. Weekly water changes of 25–30% are strongly advised given the waste produced. The Sun Catfish is classified as Endangered on the IUCN Red List due to a 60–70% population decline from overharvest, habitat modification, and pollution in its Western Ghats range (Kerala backwaters, Vembanad Lake, Chalakudy River, Meenachil River, and Nethravathi River). Dorsal and pectoral fin spines carry mild venom — handle with thick gloves and never with bare hands; the sting is painful but not considered dangerous to healthy adults. Like many catfishes (Siluriformes), this species is sensitive to copper-based medications such as copper sulfate treatments; use copper-free alternatives for disease management. The Sun Catfish is naturally gregarious and is best maintained in groups of at least five individuals of similar size, introduced simultaneously to reduce aggression.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been reliably documented in the aquarium hobby. Wild fish spawn following the monsoon season (September–December) when feeding rates peak. Any captive breeding attempt would require a very large dedicated tank with hiding places and excellent water quality; separation of eggs or fry from adults would likely be necessary. Breeding the Sun Catfish is not considered achievable under standard home aquarium conditions.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Horabagrus brachysoma
primary.notes: Primary husbandry source. Provided minimum tank size (648 L / 60″ × 24″ × 24″), water parameters (23–25°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 5–25°H), diet guidance, behaviour notes (nocturnal, peaceful for size, predatory on small fish), substrate recommendations (sand to protect barbels), native range (Kerala backwaters), and confirmation that captive breeding is unrecorded.

additional.name: FishBase — Horabagrus brachysoma
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Horabagridae, order Siluriformes), maximum size (45 cm TL), temperature range (23–25°C), native range (Kerala, India), IUCN status (Vulnerable per 2010 assessment; CAMP workshop elevated to Endangered), habitat type (demersal freshwater/brackish backwaters), and trophic level (3.1, carnivore/omnivore boundary).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Horabagrus brachysoma
additional.notes: Cross-referenced common names (mannjakoori, eclipse catfish, bullseye catfish, golden red-tail catfish), physical description (yellowish body, black shoulder spot with lighter outline, large head, four barbel pairs), native range (Kerala backwaters, Vembanad Lake, Chalakudy River, Nethravathi River, southern Karnataka), IUCN conservation status and threat drivers (overharvest, habitat alteration, pollution, 60–70% population decline).

additional.name: Fish Laboratory — Sun Catfish (Horabagrus brachysoma) Ultimate Care Guide
additional.notes: Supplementary hobbyist source. Corroborated temperature range (23–26°C), pH 6.0–8.0, minimum tank 125 gal (approx. 473 L), carnivore diet (fish, insects, crustaceans, worms), nocturnal behaviour, group size recommendation (≥5), lifespan estimate (up to 10 years), and IUCN Endangered classification per regional CAMP assessment.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Sun Catfish, Horabagrus brachysoma
additional.notes: Trade-press source. Corroborated large adult size (~30 cm aquarium typical, 45 cm museum record), nocturnal nature, 648 L minimum for single specimen, diet (dried pellets, frozen prawns, mussels, earthworms), and tankmate guidance (large cyprinids, characins, arowana, polypterus, datnioides, cichla).

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been recorded in the aquarium hobby. Wild spawning occurs following the monsoon season (September–December). Sexual dimorphism is poorly documented, though males may show a slightly elongated anal fin. Breeding is not considered achievable under standard home aquarium conditions.
